narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 37 and a high family income bracket, you've put together a strong profile in several areas, including planning to attend law school full-time like the vast majority of students. However, the admissions model predicted an 82% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is the key benchmark used to evaluate applications—leading directly to the rejection of your law school admission. This prediction hinges on a few critical factors from your academic record that stood out as the strongest influences. The most significant was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is well below the typical student's average of around 5.5; this low performance signaled to the model a heightened risk of struggling on the bar, pushing the prediction strongly toward failure. Next in influence was your LSAT score, which, while slightly above the average of 36.7, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction somewhat toward passing success. Rounding out the top factors, your Year 1 decile of 2—again lower than the average of about 5.7—further reinforced concerns about inconsistent early academic momentum, contributing toward the failure outlook. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your undergraduate GPA of 2.9 falls short of the average 3.21, though it wasn't the dominant driver here. These academic decile rankings carried the most weight because they reflect sustained performance patterns that the model sees as predictors of bar exam outcomes. While your family's financial stability and full-time commitment are positives shared by most successful applicants, they couldn't offset the early and later grade concerns. To move forward, consider retaking courses or targeted tutoring to boost your decile rankings if possible, or explore programs with bar passage support tailored for students starting from similar academic positions. Building a stronger GPA through post-baccalaureate work could also help in future applications. Many students in your situation have succeeded by addressing these gaps head-on—your LSAT strength shows real potential waiting to shine. Keep pushing; reapplying with improved academics could shift the model's view dramatically.
